title: Car theft falls in every state but one and insurers are angry
full_text:
New industry data shows insurance claims for car thefts have surged by 25 per cent in Victoria but declined in the rest of the country, and the $243 million total bill in that state exceeds the figure for all the other states combined.
The Insurance Council of Australia data shows 10,000 insurance claims for car theft worth $205 million were lodged in Victorian metropolitan regions last year, up 30 per cent by volume and 42 per cent by value, compared with the previous year.
Western Australia, South Australia and Queensland recorded significant reductions in motor theft claims - down 15 per cent, 14 per cent and 12 per cent, respectively. Queensland recorded its largest percentage decrease on record. NSW car crime claims volumes were relatively stable, down 1.6 per cent following a 3.7 per cent increase the previous year. The council does not collect data from Tasmania, the ACT or the Northern Territory.
The data comes amid rising concerns about crime in Victoria, from a surge last year in shoplifting and violence towards staff to a spate of drive-by shootings and arson attacks on Melbourne pubs and clubs this year.
Premier Jacinta Allan, under pressure to respond to worries about crime as the state heads towards an election on November 28, last year passed new bail and sentencing laws, including so-called "adult time for violent crime".
"Victoria Police is actively targeting this offending - seizing more than 800 key cloning devices in the past year alone, arresting prolific thieves and recovering high numbers of cars," a government spokesman said.
"We've also increased penalties, strengthening bail laws and made sure offenders are far more likely to be remanded."
But Opposition Leader Jess Wilson has promised to go further to toughen bail and sentencing laws if the Victorian Liberal Party wins government.
The car theft figures do not shock Sophie Day. Her Toyota Kluger was stolen from outside the family home in Kew East in February, and she said some of her neighbours had suffered the same fate.
The car was later located - minus three child car seats in the back - but is still being held by her insurance company, Budget Direct, pending her claim being finalised.
"We just woke up and the car was gone, it was stolen at about 3am; they just used one of those illegal key fob signal extenders," she said.
"We called the police who did not seem that interested. I understand it was just another one to add to a long list. The insurance company paid for a hire car but only for a couple of weeks, so we have had to borrow one."
Theft claim frequency (the incidence of motor vehicle theft as a percentage of total motor policies) fell across all states last year except in Victoria, where it rose 31 per cent, according to the Insurance Council data. The surge has been blamed for big share price falls at insurers IAG and Suncorp.
"A car is stolen or broken into every 42 minutes in Victoria," council chief executive Andrew Hall said.
"This level of crime is not acceptable. Each year, Victoria's numbers stand apart from the rest of the country, and that gap is widening."
Victoria Police said its investigations showed more than 10,000 cars per year were being stolen using devices that clone or mimic car keys. The devices, easily available for purchase online, override in-built car security systems.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Patrick Durkin
heading: General Insurance
